2026-02-13 update

- New resolved rows: Navalny toxin = epibatidine; Italian high-speed rail section = Rome and Naples. Both were bad misses. Lessons: for poisoning/toxin rows, keep a tail on obscure exact toxins instead of overconcentrating on the historically famous agent. For Italian rail sabotage rows, first disruption headlines can be a decoy or knock-on route; include the major Rome-Naples high-speed trunk when authorities investigate broader HSR cable fires.

- T20 World Cup: Zimbabwe shocked depleted Australia by 23 runs in Colombo, damaging Australia's Super Eight path. Added Australia as a major candidate for the former-champion-fails row, lowered Australia for Super Eights Group 1 first, and kept India/West Indies/South Africa ahead. India-Pakistan is scheduled for Feb 15 with heavy rain risk; since Pakistan already beat the USA and that win may be enough in the qualification path, raised United States further for the "defeat to secure berth" row while keeping India live if the India match clinches it.
- Greece migration: Reuters/Straits Times reported the deadly migrant-boat capsize occurred off Chios. Chios was not in the prior aggregate, so moved it to high confidence for the Greek-island row.
- Bangladesh/Olympics: BNP and allies claimed a large majority, reinforcing BNP-linked women for the only-woman full cabinet minister row. Italy's Olympic medal pace and Lollobrigida's second gold strengthen Italy's chance to finish exactly at 30 medals and set a national record; moved Italy level with Canada. The US men's hockey team beat Latvia 5-1, so raised the United States closer to Canada for hockey gold.

2026-02-14 update

- No new resolved rows; state.json remains absent. market.csv still has 175 active and 156 resolved rows.
- T20 World Cup: England beat Scotland, so lowered England's failure risk and shifted the former-champion-fails row toward Australia and Sri Lanka. South Africa beat New Zealand by seven wickets to go 3-0 in Group D; if Pakistan's Super Eight opener uses actual D1/D2 standings rather than fixed seed labels, South Africa is now more likely than New Zealand. Raised South Africa for Pakistan's opener and for Super Eights Group 1 first. Aiden Markram captained and made 86 not out, reinforcing him as the likely South Africa T20 tour captain.
- US-Iran: Reuters/AFP reporting says Oman will mediate/host US-Iran talks in Geneva next week, with separate talks scheduled Tuesday. Kept Oman as mediator-host but raised Switzerland/Geneva substantially for the March host/city and final-collapse rows. US military planning reports mention prior Iranian retaliation on a US base in Qatar, so raised Qatar for later Gulf-base casualty and missile-distribution rows.
- Olympics: Canada beat Switzerland 5-1 in men's hockey, the US beat Denmark 6-3, and Finland beat Sweden; Canada remains the gold favorite, with the US close and Sweden a bit lower. Italy continues to sit near the top of the medal table while the US adds speed-skating golds; raised Italy for the exact 30-medal national-record row while keeping Canada close.
- Targeted Feb 14 searches for the Pakistan motorcycle bomb, Sudan crowded-market strike, Israel bobsleigh broadcaster, Rousey opponent, Benfica remark, storm naming, Bangladesh cabinet, Barcelona hamstring injury, France-US row, Democratic response, and Dutch AI ruling returned no new direct evidence.

2026-02-15 update

- New resolved rows: Pakistan's clinching opponent = Namibia; northwest Pakistan motorcycle-bomb district = Bannu; Sudan crowded-market drone-strike town = Sodari. Missed Namibia after over-weighting the already-played USA win; lesson: if criteria ask which opponent the team "will defeat to secure" qualification, prefer the future formal clinching fixture over a prior helpful win. Bannu was in the forecast but low; keep Bannu/Lakki Marwat/Dera Ismail Khan as core northwest Pakistan district priors. Sodari was a miss; for Kordofan/central Sudan market-strike rows include smaller North Kordofan towns such as Sodari/Sudari, not only El Obeid, Um Rawaba, Er-Rahad, Kadugli, and Dilling.
- state.json remains absent. market.csv now has 172 active and 159 resolved rows.
- T20 World Cup: India beat Pakistan by 61 runs and the USA beat Namibia, while West Indies became the first team into the Super Eights. Lowered Pakistan and West Indies in the former-champion-fails row and shifted risk toward Australia and Sri Lanka, with England now less exposed after beating Scotland. Raised India, West Indies, and South Africa for Super Eights Group 1 first; added West Indies as a serious possible semifinal opponent for South Africa.
- Bangladesh: Tarique Rahman is reportedly set to be sworn in with the new cabinet on Feb 17. No direct cabinet-name article yet; kept Rumeen Farhana and Shama Obaid as the leading BNP-linked women and nudged them up slightly.
- Olympics: Italy reached 22 medals, surpassing its previous Winter record with seven competition days left, making Italy the clearest candidate to finish exactly at 30 medals while setting a national record. Raised Italy sharply over Canada for the 30-medal row.
- US-Iran: Iran's deputy foreign minister confirmed a second round of talks in Geneva on Tuesday with Omani mediation. Shifted the city rows further toward Geneva while keeping Oman as the likely country/mediator answer where the criteria frame the host as the mediator.

2026-02-16 update

- New resolved rows: Israel bobsleigh commentary broadcaster = RTS; Bangladesh only woman full cabinet minister = Afroza Khanam Rita; former T20 champion failing to reach Super Eight = Australia; Rousey's MMA opponent = Gina Carano; Western Europe/France flooding storm = Storm Pedro; St Petersburg military police blast town = Sertolovo; Benfica player accused by Vinicius = Gianluca Prestianni. Good hit: Australia. Partial hit: Sertolovo was in the tail. Miss lessons: include local/non-English public broadcasters such as RTS for Olympics broadcast rows; Bangladesh cabinet exact-person rows can resolve to low-profile BNP figures not visible in campaign coverage; Rousey return rows can use celebrity/MMA-crossover names like Gina Carano; storm-name rows need broader named-system tails beyond the current storm train; Benfica player rows should include young/less famous first-team attackers such as Prestianni, not only senior defenders and midfielders.
- state.json remains absent. market.csv now has 165 active and 166 resolved rows.
- T20 World Cup: Sri Lanka beat Australia by eight wickets and are through; England survived Italy and reached the Super Eights; Afghanistan beat UAE but still needs a Canada upset over New Zealand. South Africa are confirmed in from Group D and New Zealand look likely second, so raised South Africa for Pakistan's Super Eights opener and kept New Zealand as the fixed-seed hedge. For Super Eights Group 1 first, kept India narrowly ahead of West Indies and South Africa, with Sri Lanka as the main Group B replacement after Australia's exit.
- Olympics: Italy's record-setting 22 medals and second place behind Norway strengthen the exact-30-medal case, though overshooting remains a risk. Canada earned top men's hockey seed with a 10-2 win over France, while the US is second seed and Sweden must play in the qualification round; raised Canada for hockey gold and lowered Sweden.
- US-Iran: Geneva is repeatedly confirmed as the next talks venue while Oman mediates/hosts diplomatically. Moved the city rows further toward Geneva and left the country-host row split between Oman and Switzerland because the reporting wording is ambiguous.

2026-02-17 update

- New resolved rows: Pakistan's Super Eights opener = New Zealand; Iranian frigate sunk off Sri Lanka = IRIS Dena. Both were misses. Lessons: T20 World Cup Super Eight fixtures are using pre-set/fixed seed labels rather than actual group winners, so do not overrule fixture slot logic with live standings unless the official table says it changed. For Iranian Navy exact-ship rows, include newer Moudge-class frigates such as IRIS Dena, not only Sahand/Alborz/Jamaran.
- state.json remains absent. market.csv now has 163 active and 168 resolved rows.
- US-Iran: the second indirect round in Geneva ended with Iran saying the sides reached guiding principles, but Iran simultaneously held live-fire drills and temporarily closed the Strait of Hormuz. Raised Switzerland/Geneva for follow-on talks/final-collapse city rows while keeping Oman close for the host-country wording. Raised Strait of Hormuz for the future projectile/ship-fire waterway row and Iranian airspace for airline-resumption rows.
- AI/deepfake regulation: Ireland opened a formal DPC inquiry into X/Grok over non-consensual nudification of real people, while Spain said it would probe X, Meta, and TikTok over AI-generated child sexual abuse material. This strongly reinforces Grok as the likely tool in the Dutch-court sexual-imagery row.
- Epstein: fresh Epstein-file fallout continued, with Bill Gates facing cancelled India invitation coverage and older bipartisan House interest in a Gates subpoena still the clearest specific subpoena signal. Added Peter Mandelson as a smaller tail after House Democrats asked him to submit for questioning.
- T20/Olympics: No direct Super Eights group composition article yet beyond New Zealand resolving for Pakistan's opener. Keep fixed-slot uncertainty in mind for Group 1 and semifinal rows. Canada and the US remain the men's hockey favorites; Italy remains the leading exact-30-medal candidate but could overshoot.

2026-02-18 update

- New resolved rows: France-US reposted-comments source = Bureau of Counterterrorism; Democratic State of the Union response = Abigail Spanberger; Dutch court AI tool = Grok. Good hit: Grok. Miss lessons: for US diplomatic/social-media exact-source rows, include named State Department bureaus such as the Bureau of Counterterrorism, not only embassies and top offices. For Democratic response rows, include swing-state governors/governors-elect such as Abigail Spanberger, not just incumbent governors and congressional leaders.
- state.json remains absent. market.csv now has 160 active and 171 resolved rows.
- T20 World Cup: Hindustan Times explicitly listed Super Eight Group 1 as India, Zimbabwe, West Indies, and South Africa, and Group 2 as Pakistan, New Zealand, England, and Sri Lanka. Corrected the Group 1 leader row to remove Sri Lanka/England and add Zimbabwe. Corrected the South Africa semifinal-opponent row toward Group 2 teams: Pakistan, England, New Zealand, and Sri Lanka.
- South Africa/France rows: Al Jazeera called the current Proteas team "Aiden Markram's side" and "skipper Aiden Markram", so raised Markram for the New Zealand T20 tour captain. The Bureau of Counterterrorism resolution reinforces that the Deranque diplomatic row is US-France; raised Charles Kushner as the ambassador France is likely to summon.
- Olympics: Canada, Finland, and Slovakia reached the men's hockey semifinals while the US-Sweden quarterfinal was still pending in the available corpus. Raised Finland and Slovakia from low tails while leaving Canada first and the US second. Italy remains first for the exact-30-medal national-record row, but no direct new medal-count table was available.
- UK/Champions League: The Gorton and Denton article named the real candidates: Labour's Angeliki Stogia, Reform UK's Matt Goodwin, and the Green Party's Hannah Spencer. Replaced old-MP guesses with those names. Newcastle's 6-1 first-leg playoff win over Qarabag all but put them through; Independent coverage says either Barcelona or Chelsea awaits the winner, so shifted Newcastle's round-of-16 opponent forecast to Barcelona/Chelsea.

2026-02-19 update

- New resolved rows: Lebanon command centre armed group = Hamas; South Africa T20 tour captain = Keshav Maharaj. Both were misses. Lessons: Israeli strike rows in Lebanon can identify Palestinian factions operating from Lebanon, not only Hezbollah; keep Hamas materially live for "command centre" language. For post-World Cup bilateral tour captain rows, expect rest/rotation and stand-in leaders; Keshav Maharaj needs a much larger tail whenever South Africa may rest Aiden Markram.
- state.json remains absent. market.csv now has 158 active and 173 resolved rows.
- Men's Olympic hockey: the quarter-finals are now set/finished with US beating Sweden 2-1 in overtime, Canada-Finland and US-Slovakia as semifinals. Removed Sweden and raised the US sharply because it has the easier semifinal, while Canada remains narrow first and Finland is a live defending-champion threat.
- T20 World Cup: Al Jazeera confirmed Group 1 as India, South Africa, West Indies, Zimbabwe and Group 2 as England, New Zealand, Pakistan, Sri Lanka, with no carryover points or NRR. Zimbabwe beat Sri Lanka and West Indies finished unbeaten, so reduced India slightly for Group 1 first by Feb 25 and raised West Indies/Zimbabwe; lowered Sri Lanka for South Africa's likely semifinal opponent.
- Iran/UK escalation: major outlets reported the UK is blocking use of Diego Garcia/RAF Fairford for Iran strikes and Trump has attacked the Chagos deal. Added United Kingdom as the clear leader for the trade-cutoff-over-base-access row. Trump also gave Iran roughly a 10-15 day deadline while US forces build up, so raised Geneva as the likely "final talks collapsed" city and raised Strait of Hormuz for future ship-fire/waterway disruption.
- World Cup/Iran rows: rising US-Iran conflict increases Iran as the country likely to say it cannot participate or seek venue changes. Added US-Iran conflict/war wording for the conflict-name row and added Canada/Mexico as plausible alternative 2026 host countries for Iran's group matches, because a move within the official host set is more administratively plausible than moving to Gulf countries.

2026-02-20 update

- New resolved rows: Afghan provinces hit by Pakistan air raids = Nangarhar and Paktika; Greek island migrant-boat capsize = Crete. Both were misses. Lessons: for Pakistan-Afghanistan border air raids, include Nangarhar in the core pairs with Paktika/Khost/Kunar. For Greek migrant-boat capsizes, do not over-anchor on one Reuters island row; Crete remains a high-volume migrant route and needs a bigger tail even when Chios/Lesbos look current.
- state.json remains absent. market.csv now has 156 active and 175 resolved rows.
- Men's Olympic hockey: local articles show Canada beat Finland and the US routed Slovakia, making the final Canada vs United States. Canada needed another comeback and Crosby was out but may return; the US looked cleaner against Slovakia. Moved the hockey-gold row to a near coin flip with Canada just ahead.
- T20 World Cup: Hindustan Times powerplay analysis says South Africa have controlled dominance, India remain volatile against finger spin, and West Indies' bowling numbers are strong. Mohammad Amir publicly predicted India will miss the semifinals and picked Pakistan/New Zealand from Group 2. Shifted Group 1 first toward West Indies/South Africa over India, and moved South Africa's semifinal opponent toward Pakistan/New Zealand over England/Sri Lanka.
- Pakistan/Afghanistan: the air-raid resolution makes Afghanistan the obvious government that would "strongly condemn" Pakistan's strikes; raised Afghanistan materially for that follow-on row.
- US-Iran: CBS described the talks as Oman-mediated and said Iran is offering investment proposals as an off-ramp. Raised Oman slightly over Switzerland for the March 2 host-country row while keeping Geneva/Switzerland live for physical venue wording. UK base-access reporting persisted, so raised United Kingdom further for the trade-cutoff row.

2026-02-21 update

- New resolved rows: Ukraine double-blast city = Lviv; exact 30-medal Winter Olympics country = Italy; men's hockey gold = United States; Kurdish alliance letters = CPFIK; government strongly condemning Pakistan air strikes = India; summoned ambassador = Charles Kushner. Hits: Italy and Charles Kushner. Decent late hockey read: US was at 0.47 but Canada was still top. Miss lessons: Ukraine strike exact-city rows can resolve to western cities like Lviv, not only Kharkiv/Sumy/Dnipro. Iranian Kurdish alliance acronym rows can use unexpected formal umbrella acronyms such as CPFIK; avoid low-confidence generic acronyms like KFA/KDA. "Strongly condemns Pakistan" rows may be India as an external geopolitical critic, not the directly attacked Afghan government.
- state.json remains absent. market.csv now has 150 active and 181 resolved rows.
- T20 World Cup: Pakistan-New Zealand's Super Eights opener was washed out with one point each. That keeps Pakistan and New Zealand viable but opens Group 2 for England; moved the South Africa semifinal-opponent row to a Pakistan/New Zealand/England cluster. Al Jazeera still calls India clear favourites against South Africa, so moved India back narrowly first for Group 1 first by Feb 25 while keeping West Indies close.
- Gorton and Denton: the first constituency poll put Green Hannah Spencer 33, Reform's Matt Goodwin 29, and Labour's Angeliki Stogia 26, all within the margin. Replaced the Labour-led prior with Spencer/Goodwin/Stogia roughly in poll order.
- US-Iran: Pezeshkian said Iran will not bow to pressure, a counterproposal may be ready in days, and US reporting says military plans could target Khamenei/leadership while the IRGC threatens Hormuz and US bases. Raised Geneva for technical/final talks, raised Hormuz and Qatar-target scenarios, and updated interim-leadership rows toward Guardian Council clerics rather than general regime figures.

2026-02-22 update

- New resolved row: T20 Super Eights Group 1 leader by Feb 25 = West Indies. My final forecast had India narrowly over West Indies despite earlier West Indies strength. Lesson: once Super Eight groups are active, NRR and immediate fixture order can make a side first by a dated cutoff even if it is not the overall group favourite.
- state.json remains absent. market.csv now has 149 active and 182 resolved rows.
- US-Iran: Oman publicly confirmed the next US-Iran negotiations are set for Geneva on Thursday, while Reuters/AFP-style reporting says new talks are planned in early March and US pressure is rising. Shifted the March host/city/final-collapse rows strongly toward Switzerland/Geneva, leaving Oman as mediator/host-wording hedge.
- Iran escalation: Al Jazeera centered the Strait of Hormuz as Iran's warning channel, including live-fire drills and a temporary closure of sections of the strait; separate reporting again pointed to Qatar as the precedent for Iranian strikes on US bases, while Fox noted US aircraft at Saudi bases. Raised Hormuz and Qatar, with Saudi added to the US-troop-base casualty hedge.
- T20 World Cup: South Africa beat India by 76 runs and England crushed Sri Lanka by 51 runs. England now needs only one win against Pakistan or New Zealand to likely seal a semifin

--- 2026-02-23 www.khaleejtimes.com Hetmyer runs rampage as West Indies crush Zimbabwe in T20 World Cup
Shimron Hetmyer scored a 19-ball half-century as his scorching knock of 85 led West Indies to a dominant 107-run win over Zimbabwe in their first Super Eights match at the Twenty20 World Cup, continuing the two-time champions' winning run. Hetmyer scored the fastest fifty by a West Indian at a T20 World Cup as the two-time champions posted a mammoth total of 254-6 in Mumbai, the second-highest total in the tournament's history after Sri Lanka's 260-6 against Kenya in 2007. Zimbabwe were all out for 147 as Gudakesh Motie took four wickets, sending two-time champions West Indies to the top of the Group One table thanks to a better net run rate than South Africa, who had beaten India on Sunday. Zimbabwe's decision to bowl first initially paid off with opener Brandon King falling in the third over. Blessing Muzarabani almost got Hetmyer out in the next over, but Tashinga Musekiwa dropped a flat pull shot at long on. Player-of-the-match Hetmyer hit five sixes in the last six deliveries he faced before completing his half-century in the eighth over, with West Indies looking strong at 92-2. Musekiwa dropped Hetmyer again at the same position in the 12th over, when the 29-year-old had scored 70 runs. Rovman Powell (59 off 35 balls) stood by Hetmyer to add 122 runs for the third wicket, until Hetmyer finally fell in the 15th over, having hit seven fours and seven sixes to score 85 off 34 balls, his personal best in T20 internationals. Sherfane Rutherford piled further misery on Zimbabwe, scoring an unbeaten 31 from 13 balls as West Indies added 46 runs in their last three overs. Needing to chase a tournament-record 255, Zimbabwe lost three batters within five deliveries when Akeal Hosein took two wickets in a maiden over to leave the African side at 20-3 after three overs. Dion Myers (28 off 15 balls) put up a fight, but was bowled by a flighted ball from spinner Motie (4-28), with Zimbabwe 52-4 in the seventh over. Motie also bowled Zimbabwe captain Sikandar Raza (27) and Musekiwa in back-to-back deliveries, before getting Tony Munyonga (14) caught at deep midwicket, demolishing the middle order. Facing certain defeat, Brad Evans made a defiant last stand, scoring a quick 43 to reduce the Caribbean side's margin of victory as Zimbabwe suffered their first loss in the campaign. Zimbabwe will face defending champions India in a battle to bounce back on Thursday, the same day West Indies play last edition's finalists South Africa.

Pakistan, despite being humiliated by India in the group phase, possess a stacked roster who, on their day, can compete with any cricket team in the world. England, listed as the pre-tournament joint-second favorite to raise the T20 world crown, are slowly building momentum in the competition, as exhibited by their dismantling of host nation Sri Lanka in their Super Eight opener on Sunday. Al Jazeera Sport takes a look at the matchup, which may turn out to be a pivotal outcome affecting both nations’ semifinal qualification hopes. A defeat would put England, who skittled Sri Lanka out for just 95 runs, through to the semifinals with a game to spare. Pakistan would then need to beat Sri Lanka in their final Super Eight match and hope other results go their way to reach the last four. History will be against Pakistan as they have never beaten England in three previous Twenty20 (T20) World Cup clashes. “We are confident and our morale is high,” said Pakistan batsman Sahibzada Farhan, who scored an unbeaten 100 against Namibia in Pakistan’s final group match. “We are focused on this match to win and progress.” Weather watch for Pakistan in Pallekele Persistent rain in the Sri Lankan capital Colombo forced the abandonment of Pakistan’s opening Super Eight game with New Zealand without a single ball being bowled, forcing the sides to split the points at R Premadasa Stadium. Pakistan will be hoping that the weather does not play a factor in their crucial second tie as another split result would all but end their semifinal aspirations. Thankfully, the forecast looks good for Tuesday’s match against England at the Pallekele International Stadium in Kandy, with 33 degrees Celsius (91F) predicted with only a 25 percent possibility of rain. In short, there should be a result and a full match is a strong possibility. England rounding into form England kicked off their Super Eight campaign with a 51-run victory over Sri Lanka, a statistically dominant result that vaulted them to the top of the Group 2 standings on net run rate. The two-time champions have now won their last three matches at the tournament. Will Jacks has been the breakout star with the bat at the tournament, averaging 65 on a scintillating 195 strike rate. Pakistan to put England in a spin Pakistan on Monday warned England’s inconsistent batting lineup to expect a trial by spin when the teams clash. Farhan told reporters that England struggled to 146-9 against Sri Lanka’s spinners on Sunday. The in-form opener said that England can expect more of the same from Pakistan’s spinners when they meet on the same Pallekele ground on Tuesday night. “What we saw in the Sri Lanka-England game was that the ball was gripping and England struggled against spin,” said Farhan on Monday. “Sri Lanka have one or two spinners, but we have five in all, so we will give England a tough time on a pitch that looks good and will grip,” he added. Pakistan’s spinners have taken 26 wickets in the four matches so far. Their seamers have dismissed only seven batsmen. ‘Will not be difficult’: Farhan on Archer express Farhan, who tops the T20 World Cup run-scoring chart with 220, said he was ready for the threat of England’s express pace bowler Jofra Archer. “Facing Archer will not be difficult because I have faced similar bowlers in Pakistan,” said Farhan. “So if he has plans against me, I also have plans against him.” Pakistan team news Pakistan are likely to bring in spinner Abrar Ahmed in place of seaming all-rounder Faheem Ashraf. Shaheen Shah Afridi’s omission from the final group stage match against Namibia and the Super Eight opener against New Zealand was a huge shock. The bowling superstar was expensive in the group phase, with his side pivoting to a spin-dominant strategy. With Pakistan desperately needing a win against England, the left-arm quick is expected to return to the starting XI. England team news E

The ideal scenario for India now is to win both their remaining Group 1 matches against Zimbabwe and the West Indies, and hope at the same time South Africa don't lose to any of those two teams. "Like us, they [Zimbabwe] are very confident, they are playing some good cricket. T20 is a format that brings teams closer together. There's no clear-cut winner. These guys have played well. "There are a lot of things that are going for them. "I could understand from that standpoint, where nobody gives you a chance, how you could use that as inspiration," he added. "It's the first time in a long time that we've had every single person that is available for West Indies playing. Plus, the guys believe they could do something special, that's what I saw and that's why I said it from the first press conference, I feel we could do something special."
